name: The parable of the wicked husbandmen
notes: Jesus tells the parable of the wicked husbandmen in all three synoptic gospels during the temple confrontations of His final week. The shared core is constant: a man plants a vineyard and lets it to husbandmen, sends servants at the season to receive the fruit, the husbandmen beat and kill them, and last of all he sends his beloved son, whom they cast out and slay, saying the inheritance shall be theirs; the lord will then destroy them and give the vineyard to others, and Jesus seals it with the rejected stone of Psalm 118. Matthew gives the fullest setting of the vineyard (hedge, winepress, tower) and uniquely has the hearers pronounce their own judgment and adds the kingdom given to a nation bringing forth fruit. Mark traces the servants singly and dwells on the beloved son sent last of all, while Luke compresses the vineyard's setting and uniquely records the crowd's cry of 'God forbid.' These are complementary vantages on one parable; the Psalm 118 'head of the corner' citation, shared here by all three, is also gathered in the separate stone weave. The chief priests perceive He spake of them and seek to lay hold on Him but fear the people.
